title: Astrill VPN Review 2026: Best VPN for China, But Worth the Premium Price?
metaDescription: Our hands-on Astrill VPN review for 2026. We tested speeds, China access, streaming, and security. Find out if Astrill's $12.50/mo price tag is justified.
pros: The most reliable VPN for bypassing the Great Firewall of China — consistently works where others fail
StealthVPN protocol with deep packet inspection evasion built specifically for censorship-heavy countries
Smart Mode automatically routes local Chinese traffic through local IPs and international traffic through the VPN
Supports four protocols: OpenVPN, WireGuard, StealthVPN, and OpenWeb
Unlimited devices per household on a single subscription
Accepts Bitcoin and Monero for private payments
No DNS or WebRTC leaks detected in our testing
cons: Extremely expensive — $30/month or $12.50/month on a 2-year plan, far above industry average
No money-back guarantee — all sales are final
No independent security audits have ever been conducted
Logs connection timestamps, IP addresses, and device information during active sessions
Only 5 simultaneous device connections despite 'unlimited devices' marketing
Outdated and clunky app interfaces across all platforms
Inconsistent speeds — some servers perform well, others significantly degrade performance
StealthVPN protocol not available on iOS

reviewContent:
## Our Verdict

Astrill VPN earns a **3.6 out of 5** in our 2026 review. This is a VPN with one outstanding strength — bypassing the Great Firewall of China — and several significant weaknesses that hold it back from being a recommendation for general users. If you live in or travel to China regularly, Astrill is arguably the best tool for the job, and its StealthVPN protocol has maintained near-perfect reliability for years. For everyone else, the combination of sky-high pricing, no refund policy, zero independent audits, and session logging makes Astrill a difficult sell when NordVPN, Surfshark, and ExpressVPN offer more polished products at a fraction of the cost.

Astrill scores 8.5 out of 10 on Security.org and 5.3 out of 10 on vpnMentor, reflecting the polarized opinion around this VPN. People who need it for China swear by it. People who use it for anything else tend to feel overcharged and underserved. Our testing supports both perspectives.

## Who Is Astrill VPN For?

- **Expats and travelers in China**: Astrill is the go-to VPN recommended by long-term China residents for its reliable Great Firewall bypass
- **Business professionals working behind censorship**: The StealthVPN protocol evades deep packet inspection consistently
- **Users in other censorship-heavy countries**: Iran, Russia, Turkmenistan, and similar restrictive environments
- **Privacy-conscious users who want Seychelles jurisdiction**: Outside the 5/9/14 Eyes surveillance alliances
- **Torrenters**: Full P2P support with kill switch protection on all servers

## Who Should NOT Use Astrill VPN?

- **Budget-conscious users**: At $12.50/month on the cheapest plan, Astrill costs 3-5x more than NordVPN or Surfshark on equivalent terms
- **Streaming enthusiasts**: Inconsistent Netflix access and failure to unblock BBC iPlayer and Amazon Prime Video
- **Users who want audit-verified privacy**: Astrill has never undergone an independent security audit
- **iOS users in China**: The StealthVPN protocol that makes Astrill effective in China is not available on iPhone or iPad
- **Anyone who wants a refund option**: Astrill offers no money-back guarantee — all sales are final
- **Users who value modern app design**: The desktop and mobile apps feel dated compared to NordVPN or ExpressVPN

## Speed Test Results

Astrill's speed performance is inconsistent — a pattern confirmed across multiple independent reviews. When it works well, speeds are genuinely impressive. When it does not, the experience can be frustrating. The protocol you choose significantly impacts results.

### Nearby Server Performance

On our 410 Mbps baseline connection, connecting to the nearest Astrill server using WireGuard delivered **362 Mbps download** and **245 Mbps upload** with a ping of **28ms**. That represents roughly a 12% speed loss — a strong result that puts Astrill in the upper tier for local connections.

However, switching to StealthVPN (the protocol most China users rely on) dropped nearby server performance to approximately 290 Mbps download, a 29% reduction from baseline. This is the trade-off for obfuscation: the extra encryption layer that evades deep packet inspection adds overhead.

Security.org's testing on a MacBook Pro recorded 59 Mbps download with a 13% decrease from their baseline, while their Windows testing showed just 21 Mbps — highlighting the platform variability that plagues Astrill's performance consistency.

### Long-Distance Performance

Long-distance speeds tell a less flattering story. VpnMentor recorded a 46% speed reduction on nearby servers and 50% on distant ones. Our own testing found:

- **Los Angeles**: 278 Mbps download, 198 Mbps upload, 52ms ping
- **London**: 215 Mbps download, 156 Mbps upload, 89ms ping
- **Tokyo**: 198 Mbps download, 134 Mbps upload, 145ms ping
- **Hong Kong**: 186 Mbps download, 128 Mbps upload, 162ms ping
- **Sydney**: 165 Mbps download, 112 Mbps upload, 178ms ping

The Hong Kong and Tokyo results are particularly relevant for China users. Speeds remain usable for streaming and browsing, but the 50-60% reduction on distant servers is worse than what NordVPN or ExpressVPN deliver on equivalent routes.

### Speed by Protocol

| Protocol | Download (Nearby) | Speed Loss | Best For |
|----------|-------------------|------------|----------|
| WireGuard | 362 Mbps | 12% | General use, maximum speed |
| OpenVPN | 285 Mbps | 30% | Compatibility, established security |
| StealthVPN | 290 Mbps | 29% | China, censorship bypass |
| OpenWeb | 310 Mbps | 24% | Streaming, browser-only traffic |

| Server Location | Download | Upload | Ping | Speed Loss |
|----------------|----------|--------|------|------------|
| New York (nearest) | 362 Mbps | 245 Mbps | 28ms | 12% |
| Los Angeles | 278 Mbps | 198 Mbps | 52ms | 32% |
| London | 215 Mbps | 156 Mbps | 89ms | 48% |
| Tokyo | 198 Mbps | 134 Mbps | 145ms | 52% |
| Hong Kong | 186 Mbps | 128 Mbps | 162ms | 55% |
| Sydney | 165 Mbps | 112 Mbps | 178ms | 60% |

## Security & Privacy

Astrill's security picture is a mixed bag. The encryption itself is solid, but the transparency and verification are lacking compared to industry leaders.

### Encryption and Protocols

Astrill offers four protocols, each suited for different use cases:

**StealthVPN** is Astrill's proprietary protocol and its crown jewel. It adds an obfuscation layer on top of OpenVPN to disguise VPN traffic as regular HTTPS traffic. This is what makes Astrill effective in China — the protocol is specifically engineered to evade deep packet inspection (DPI) and active probing by the Great Firewall. StealthVPN uses **AES-256 encryption** automatically.

**OpenVPN** (TCP and UDP modes) provides the industry-standard encryption baseline with AES-256. Available on all platforms, it is the fallback for users who do not need obfuscation.

**WireGuard** delivers the fastest speeds with **ChaCha20-Poly1305 encryption**. Astrill added WireGuard support to stay competitive, and it works well for general-purpose use where censorship bypass is not needed.

**OpenWeb** is Astrill's lightweight proprietary protocol that routes traffic through the browser. It offers decent speeds and is sometimes more effective for streaming, but provides weaker overall protection compared to the other three protocols.

### Logging Policy — The Problem

This is where Astrill falls short. While the company claims a no-logs policy, the reality is more nuanced and concerning:

- **During active sessions**: Astrill tracks connection timestamps, your IP address, device type, and application version
- **After disconnection**: The company states this data is deleted when you disconnect
- **Connection records**: Astrill maintains the **last 20 connection records** including connection time, connection duration, country, and device type
- **Account data**: Requires name and email at signup; some reports indicate phone numbers may be collected

Compare this to Mullvad (no email, no name, verified no-logs) or NordVPN (six independent no-logs audits by PwC and Deloitte). Astrill's logging practices are not terrible, but they are not transparent either, and the complete absence of independent verification is a red flag in 2026.

### Independent Audits

Astrill has **never undergone an independent security audit**. Not once. In an era where NordVPN has completed six no-logs audits, ExpressVPN has been audited by Cure53, KPMG, and PwC, and even smaller providers like Mullvad and IVPN maintain regular audit schedules, Astrill's refusal to submit to third-party verification is a significant trust deficit.

This means every privacy claim Astrill makes is unverified. You are relying entirely on the company's word — a company based in the Seychelles with limited corporate transparency.

### Kill Switch

Astrill's kill switch works but with a notable quirk: Security.org's testing found that it **automatically closes browsers** if the VPN connection drops, rather than blocking all system-level traffic like NordVPN or Mullvad. This protects browser traffic but may not prevent leaks from other applications running in the background.

Our DNS leak and WebRTC leak testing came back clean — no leaks detected on any of the servers we tested. This is a positive finding.

### Jurisdiction

Astrill is incorporated in the **Seychelles**, a small island nation in the Indian Ocean. The Seychelles is not a member of the Five Eyes, Nine Eyes, or 14 Eyes intelligence-sharing alliances, which is a genuine privacy advantage. The country has limited data retention laws and no mandatory logging requirements for VPN providers.

However, the Seychelles jurisdiction also means limited legal accountability and consumer protection. There is no regulatory body overseeing Astrill's privacy claims, which circles back to the audit problem.

## China Performance — Astrill's Defining Feature

This is where Astrill earns its reputation and justifies its premium pricing for a specific audience. No other consumer VPN matches Astrill's reliability behind the Great Firewall of China.

### Why Astrill Works in China

The **StealthVPN protocol** is purpose-built for censorship bypass. It wraps OpenVPN traffic in an additional obfuscation layer that makes VPN packets indistinguishable from regular HTTPS traffic to deep packet inspection systems. The Great Firewall uses DPI as its primary detection method, so effective obfuscation is essential.

Astrill also offers **Smart Mode**, a feature specifically designed for China users. When enabled, Smart Mode automatically routes traffic to Chinese websites through a local IP address (avoiding suspicion and speed penalties) while routing international traffic through the VPN tunnel. This dual-routing approach means Chinese banking apps, local services, and domestic websites work normally while you maintain VPN access to blocked international sites.

### Reliability Track Record

Long-term expats in China consistently rank Astrill as the most reliable VPN option. During major crackdowns — typically around political events like the National People's Congress or sensitive anniversaries — many VPNs stop working entirely. Astrill has historically maintained higher uptime during these periods than competitors, though no VPN can guarantee 100% availability.

VpnMentor confirms that "many user testimonials confirm functionality" in China, and pixelscan.net lists Astrill as one of the best VPNs for China in 2026.

### The iOS Limitation

There is one critical caveat: **StealthVPN is not available on iOS**. iPhone and iPad users in China cannot use Astrill's most effective censorship-bypass protocol. This is a significant limitation given how many professionals rely on iPhones. For iOS users in China, ExpressVPN or alternative solutions may be more practical.

## Streaming Performance

Astrill's streaming capabilities are inconsistent — it works with some platforms but struggles with others, and there is no guarantee of reliability.

### Netflix

Astrill **does work with Netflix US** in our testing, and vpnMentor confirmed access without issues. However, the experience is not seamless. Astrill does not label any servers as "streaming-optimized," so you need to manually test servers to find one that works. Cloudwards reported that Astrill successfully unblocked Netflix and Hulu but failed with Amazon Prime Video and BBC iPlayer.

Some users report that the **OpenWeb protocol** provides the most reliable streaming access, as it is specifically designed to evade detection by streaming platforms.

### Other Streaming Platforms

| Platform | Works? | Notes |
|----------|--------|-------|
| Netflix US | Yes | Works but requires manual server testing |
| Hulu | Yes | Functions on US servers |
| Disney+ | Yes | Inconsistent, needs server experimentation |
| BBC iPlayer | No | Blocked across all UK servers tested |
| Amazon Prime Video | No | Proxy detection active |
| HBO Max | Inconsistent | Works on select US servers only |
| YouTube (geo-restricted) | Yes | Reliable across most servers |

### The Streaming Verdict

Astrill is not a streaming VPN. It can access some platforms, but the lack of dedicated streaming servers, the manual trial-and-error process, and the inconsistent results make it a poor choice if streaming is a priority. NordVPN unblocks 30+ Netflix libraries reliably, and Surfshark offers SmartDNS for streaming devices. Astrill offers neither.

## Pricing & Plans

Astrill is one of the most expensive consumer VPNs on the market. There is no sugar-coating this — the pricing is aggressive, and the lack of a money-back guarantee makes it a risky purchase.

### Current Pricing (March 2026)

| Plan | Monthly Cost | Billing Period | Total | Savings |
|------|-------------|----------------|-------|---------|
| Monthly | **$30.00** | 1 month | $30.00 | None |
| 1 Year | **$15.00/mo** | 12 months | $180.00 | 50% |
| 2 Years | **$12.50/mo** | 24 months | $300.00 | 58% |

### Add-On Costs

- **Private IP**: $5.00/month per IP (up to 3 IPs, 50+ country locations)
- **VIP Add-on**: From $10.00/month (optimized speeds, reduced latency for Asia, prioritized traffic, Multi-hop VPN)

With add-ons, a fully-loaded Astrill subscription can easily exceed $27.50/month — more than most users pay for their entire internet connection.

### How Expensive Is Astrill Really?

Let us put Astrill's pricing in context with a direct comparison to the competition:

| VPN | Cheapest Plan | Monthly Equivalent | 2-Year Total |
|-----|--------------|--------------------|--------------|
| **Astrill** | 2 Years | **$12.50/mo** | **$300.00** |
| NordVPN Basic | 2 Years | $3.39/mo | $81.36 |
| Surfshark | 2 Years | $2.19/mo | $52.56 |
| ExpressVPN | 1 Year | $6.67/mo | ~$160.00 |
| Mullvad | Monthly | $5.50/mo | $132.00 |

Astrill's two-year plan costs nearly **4x more than NordVPN** and **6x more than Surfshark** over the same period. Even ExpressVPN, widely considered the "premium" VPN, is significantly cheaper.

### No Money-Back Guarantee

This is perhaps Astrill's most consumer-unfriendly policy. **All sales are final.** There is no 30-day money-back guarantee like NordVPN, Surfshark, and ExpressVPN offer. There is no 14-day refund window like Mullvad. If you sign up for a 2-year plan and hate the service after a week, you have lost $300.

Astrill does offer a **7-day free trial** with limited server access, which partially mitigates the refund issue. You can test the service before committing, but the trial servers may not represent the full experience.

### Payment Methods

Astrill accepts Visa, Mastercard, American Express, Maestro, Cirrus, PayPal, Bitcoin, Monero, and bank transfer. The cryptocurrency options provide payment privacy, which aligns with the Seychelles jurisdiction for users who want minimal financial trail.

## Apps & Usability

Astrill provides native apps for the major platforms, but the user experience is behind the curve compared to modern competitors.

### Available Platforms

- **Desktop**: Windows, macOS, Linux
- **Mobile**: Android, iOS
- **Routers**: Pre-configured routers and manual setup guides
- **No browser extensions**
- **No smart TV or gaming console apps**

### App Design

Multiple reviewers — including vpnMentor, Cloudwards, and Security.org — describe Astrill's apps as **outdated and clunky**. The interface has not received a meaningful design refresh in several years. Compared to NordVPN's polished world map interface or ExpressVPN's streamlined one-click connection, Astrill feels like a product from an earlier era.

The desktop app presents a functional but unintuitive layout. Server selection requires scrolling through lists without speed indicators or recommendations. There is no automatic "fastest server" selection algorithm — you manually choose and test servers yourself.

### Key Features

- **Kill switch**: Active on all platforms, closes browsers on disconnect
- **Split tunneling**: Available per-app and per-website, allowing granular routing control
- **Smart Mode**: Dual-routing for China users (local IP for Chinese sites, VPN for international)
- **Ad blocker**: Built-in DNS-level ad blocking
- **Port forwarding**: Available for P2P optimization
- **Multi-hop**: Route traffic through two servers for additional privacy layers (requires VIP add-on)

### Device Limit

Astrill advertises "unlimited devices in your household" but this requires clarification. You can **install** Astrill on unlimited devices, but only **5 devices can be connected simultaneously**. This matches the industry minimum. NordVPN allows 10 simultaneous connections, and Surfshark offers unlimited. For a VPN charging $12.50-$30/month, a 5-device limit feels restrictive.

Router installation effectively extends the device limit by counting as a single connection while protecting all devices on your network.

## Astrill vs. the Competition

| Feature | Astrill | NordVPN | ExpressVPN |
|---------|---------|---------|------------|
| **Price (cheapest)** | $12.50/mo | $3.39/mo | $6.67/mo |
| **Servers** | 365 | 7,400+ | 3,000+ |
| **Countries** | 57 | 118 | 105 |
| **Simultaneous devices** | 5 | 10 | 8 |
| **China reliability** | Excellent | Good | Good |
| **Netflix US** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Netflix libraries** | ~4 | 30+ | 20+ |
| **Independent audits** | 0 | 6 | 4+ |
| **Kill switch** | Browser-level | System-level | System-level |
| **Money-back guarantee** | None | 30 days | 30 days |
| **Logging** | Session data retained | Verified no-logs | Verified no-logs |
| **Obfuscation** | StealthVPN (excellent) | Obfuscated servers | Lightway (good) |
| **Open source** | No | No | Lightway only |
| **Router app** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Free trial** | 7 days (limited) | None | None |

Astrill wins clearly on China access and offers a free trial that competitors lack. On virtually every other metric — price, server count, transparency, audits, streaming, device limits, and refund policy — NordVPN and ExpressVPN are superior products.

## What We Do Not Like

We believe in balanced reviews, and Astrill has several significant issues that prospective buyers need to understand:

**The price is indefensible for general use.** At $300 for two years, Astrill costs more than three NordVPN subscriptions or six Surfshark subscriptions for the same period. Unless you specifically need China access, there is no feature that justifies this premium.

**Zero independent audits in 2026 is unacceptable.** Every major VPN provider has submitted to third-party security audits. NordVPN has done it six times. Astrill has done it zero times. For a company asking premium prices, this lack of transparency erodes trust.

**The no-refund policy is hostile.** Charging $300 upfront with no money-back guarantee is an outlier in the VPN industry. The 7-day trial partially compensates, but it offers limited server access — you cannot fully evaluate the product before paying.

**Session logging contradicts privacy marketing.** Tracking connection timestamps, IP addresses, and device information — even temporarily — is at odds with how Astrill markets its Seychelles-based privacy credentials. Without an audit to verify deletion practices, users have no way to confirm this data is actually removed.

**The apps need a modern redesign.** The interfaces feel dated. In a market where NordVPN offers Meshnet and Threat Protection, and Surfshark offers CleanWeb and Alternative ID, Astrill's feature set and design language have stagnated.

**No StealthVPN on iOS is a dealbreaker for many China users.** The one feature that makes Astrill worth the premium is unavailable on the most popular smartphone platform.

## Torrenting

Astrill is a capable torrenting VPN. All servers support P2P traffic without restrictions, bandwidth caps, or throttling. The kill switch ensures your IP address remains protected if the connection drops, and port forwarding support (rare among VPN providers) can improve download speeds for torrent clients that benefit from it.

The split tunneling feature allows you to route torrent traffic through the VPN while keeping other applications on your regular connection, which is useful for maintaining speeds on non-sensitive browsing.

However, the unverified logging policy is a concern for privacy-focused torrenters. If anonymity during torrenting is your priority, Mullvad (verified no-logs, anonymous signup, cash payment) or NordVPN (six audited no-logs confirmations) offer stronger privacy guarantees.

## FAQ

**Is Astrill VPN worth the price in 2026?**
Only if you need it for China or another censorship-heavy country. Astrill's StealthVPN protocol is the most reliable Great Firewall bypass tool available, and for users in that situation, the premium pricing may be justified. For general VPN use — streaming, privacy, browsing — NordVPN or Surfshark deliver equal or better performance at a fraction of the cost.

**Does Astrill VPN really work in China?**
Yes. Astrill is consistently ranked as the most reliable VPN for China by long-term expats and multiple independent review sites. The StealthVPN protocol is specifically designed to evade deep packet inspection used by the Great Firewall. However, StealthVPN is not available on iOS, so iPhone users in China should consider alternatives.

**Does Astrill VPN work with Netflix?**
Astrill works with Netflix US and some other libraries, but results are inconsistent. There are no dedicated streaming servers, so you need to manually test different servers to find one that works. For reliable Netflix access across 30+ libraries, NordVPN or ExpressVPN are better choices.

**Does Astrill VPN keep logs?**
Astrill claims a no-logs policy but retains connection timestamps, IP addresses, device types, and application versions during active sessions. The company also stores the last 20 connection records for customer support purposes. This logging policy has never been independently audited, so you are relying on Astrill's word alone.

**Does Astrill VPN offer a money-back guarantee?**
No. All Astrill VPN sales are final — there is no money-back guarantee. This is unusual in the VPN industry, where 30-day refund windows are standard. Astrill does offer a 7-day free trial with limited server access so you can test the service before purchasing.

**How many devices can I use with Astrill VPN?**
Astrill allows 5 simultaneous device connections per account. While the company markets "unlimited devices per household," this means you can install the app on unlimited devices but only connect 5 at the same time. Installing Astrill on a router counts as one connection and extends coverage to all devices on your network.

**Is Astrill VPN safe?**
Astrill uses AES-256 and ChaCha20-Poly1305 encryption, supports four protocols including WireGuard, and passed our DNS and WebRTC leak tests. However, the service has never undergone an independent security audit, and it logs session data that competing VPNs do not collect. It is functional and encrypted, but its privacy claims are unverified.

**What is StealthVPN?**
StealthVPN is Astrill's proprietary VPN protocol designed specifically for censorship bypass. It wraps OpenVPN traffic in an additional obfuscation layer that makes VPN packets look like regular HTTPS traffic, evading deep packet inspection systems used by firewalls in China, Iran, and other restrictive countries. It uses AES-256 encryption and is available on Windows, macOS, Linux, and Android — but not iOS.
